notes: This is a planning syllabus, not an import package.
Every grammar topic must be produced for A1-C2 coverage according to this syllabus.
Localized explanations must be authored/adapted per language, not merely translated sentence-by-sentence.
Use the stable slug/contentKey for upsert. Codex/importer should treat same slug as an update, not a duplicate.
Image assets, if any, should be referenced by stable asset keys and stored under content/learning-portal/grammar/assets/{slug}/.

targetLanguages.name: English
targetLanguages.localizationNote: Use clear learner-friendly explanations and compare with English word order when useful.

targetLanguages.name: Persian
targetLanguages.localizationNote: Explain through Persian grammar intuition; point out where German differs from Persian, especially articles, cases, and word order.

targetLanguages.name: Arabic
targetLanguages.localizationNote: Use Arabic grammar comparisons carefully; explain German case/article behavior separately from Arabic definite article logic.

targetLanguages.name: Turkish
targetLanguages.localizationNote: Compare with Turkish agglutinative structure only when helpful; emphasize German articles, case endings, and word order differences.

targetLanguages.name: Russian
targetLanguages.localizationNote: Use Russian case-system intuition where useful, but explain German article/case interaction explicitly.

targetLanguages.name: Kurdish Sorani
targetLanguages.localizationNote: Use simple Sorani explanations and avoid assuming strong grammatical terminology unless introduced.

targetLanguages.name: Kurdish Kurmanji
targetLanguages.localizationNote: Use Kurmanji-friendly explanations and highlight German article/case differences.

targetLanguages.name: Polish
targetLanguages.localizationNote: Use Polish case-system intuition where useful; clarify differences in articles and German word order.

targetLanguages.name: Romanian
targetLanguages.localizationNote: Compare article/gender concepts carefully and show German-specific article placement and cases.

targetLanguages.name: Albanian
targetLanguages.localizationNote: Use Albanian-friendly explanations and explicitly introduce German article/case concepts.

topics.titleEn: Personal pronouns: ich, du, er, sie, es
topics.titleDeSuggestion: Personalpronomen: ich, du, er, sie, es
topics.generationBrief: Introduce German subject pronouns and when learners use each pronoun in simple self-introduction sentences.
Teach the present-tense forms of sein with identity, origin, job, and simple descriptions.
Teach haben in simple possession and daily-life sentences, especially for family, time, and objects.
Explain weak verb endings in the present tense with wohnen, lernen, machen, kaufen, and arbeiten.
Teach the core German rule that the finite verb usually stands in position two in main clauses.
Show how German yes/no questions move the finite verb to the first position.

topics.titleEn: W-questions: wer, was, wo, wann, wie
topics.titleDeSuggestion: W-questions: wer, was, wo, wann, wie
topics.generationBrief: Teach common W-question words and simple question formation for daily communication.
Introduce definite articles and the idea that German nouns have grammatical gender.
Explain ein/eine for first mention and simple object descriptions.
Explain masculine, feminine, and neuter at A1 level with practical learning strategies.
Introduce common plural patterns without overwhelming beginners with all exceptions.
Explain nominative as the subject case using simple who/what-does-something examples.
Introduce accusative as the direct object with simple shopping and food examples.
Teach the first distinction between kein for nouns and nicht for verbs/adjectives/simple phrases.
Introduce mein/dein/sein/ihr for simple ownership and family/object sentences.
Show adjectives before nouns and after sein without going deeply into endings yet.

topics.titleEn: Basic prepositions: in, aus, nach, bei
topics.titleDeSuggestion: Basic prepositions: in, aus, nach, bei
topics.generationBrief: Teach high-frequency location/origin/destination prepositions for beginner communication.
Show how numbers appear with nouns, prices, times, appointments, and phone numbers.
Teach simple time adverbs and how they fit into basic German sentences.

topics.titleEn: Word order with time and place
topics.titleDeSuggestion: Word order with time and place
topics.generationBrief: Introduce simple time-place ordering and common beginner sentence patterns.

topics.titleEn: Simple modal verbs: können, müssen, wollen
topics.titleDeSuggestion: Simple modal verbs: können, müssen, wollen
topics.generationBrief: Teach the first modal verbs and the infinitive at the end in simple sentences.
Explain möchte as a polite beginner structure for ordering and requesting.
Introduce simple commands and polite requests at A1 level.
Introduce verbs such as einkaufen and aufstehen with separated prefixes in simple present tense.
Teach coordination with und and aber without changing word order.
Explain that the verb ending must match the subject pronoun.
Teach formal Sie and why the verb form is different from du.
Compare informal and formal address from a grammar and usage perspective.
Review simple negation patterns with nicht and kein in beginner sentences.
Teach reusable structures for answering common A1 questions correctly.

Introduce high-frequency irregular participles learners need for A2 conversation.

Introduce dative for indirect objects and common daily-life expressions.
Contrast accusative and dative through simple who-receives-what examples.

Introduce am schönsten and der/die/das beste at A2 level.
Introduce adjective endings in simple high-frequency article+noun patterns.
Teach polite question structures such as Können Sie mir sagen, wo ...?
